name: Holle
description: Holle, also known as Frau Holle, Holda, or Hulda, is a multifaceted goddess figure in Germanic folklore and mythology. She embodies aspects of domesticity, fertility, witchcraft, and the afterlife. Her origins are deeply rooted in pre-Christian Germanic traditions, possibly stemming from a powerful mother goddess figure. Holle is often depicted as an elderly woman with white hair, reflecting her association with winter and wisdom. She is believed to reside in a well or cave, acting as a guardian of the otherworld. Holle's powers are diverse; she controls the weather, particularly snow, which is said to fall when she shakes her feather bed. She also oversees spinning and weaving, rewarding diligent spinners and punishing the lazy. Furthermore, she is associated with childbirth and the care of children, acting as a protector of women and infants. Holle's role extends to the afterlife, where she receives the souls of the dead, particularly those who died in childbirth or infancy. She is considered both a benevolent and a fearsome figure, embodying the duality of nature and the cyclical nature of life and death. In some accounts, she leads a Wild Hunt-like procession during the winter months, further solidifying her connection to the supernatural and the dark aspects of the season. Holle represents a complex and enduring figure in Germanic folklore, reflecting the importance of nature, family, and the spiritual realm in the lives of early Germanic peoples.
subtitle: Goddess of Winter, Spinning, and the Afterlife

keyMyths.title: Frau Holle's Well
keyMyths.description: A girl who falls into Frau Holle's well is rewarded for her hard work and kindness with gold, while her lazy stepsister is punished with pitch.

keyMyths.title: Shaking the Bed
keyMyths.description: When Frau Holle shakes her feather bed, it snows on Earth, demonstrating her control over the winter weather.

keyMyths.title: Guardian of Souls
keyMyths.description: Holle receives the souls of the dead in her realm, offering comfort and guidance to those who have passed on, particularly mothers and children.

sources.author: Jacob Grimm and Wilhelm Grimm
